narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 52.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use for approval. Your profile shows a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.3, which is above the typical 3.21 average, along with an LSAT score of 35—slightly below the usual 36.7—while your school deciles were lower at 3rd in Year 1 (versus an average 5.71) and 2nd in Year 3 (compared to 5.53). You come from an upper-middle income family, identify as female, White, and plan to attend full-time, which aligns closely with many applicants. In assessing your bar exam odds, the model weighed your Year 1 school decile as the most influential factor, as your lower ranking there strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a failure. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which also nudged the outlook toward failure compared to higher-achieving peers. Your Year 3 decile ranked third in influence, similarly contributing to the failure prediction due to its below-average position in your school. Family income followed as the fourth key driver, with your upper-middle status playing a supporting role in that direction. Your undergraduate GPA, despite being a strength relative to averages, still edged the prediction slightly toward failure in the model's view, coming in fifth. Factors like race, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller influences, all leaning toward the same concerning outcome. Overall, your early and later school performance stood out as the top concerns, setting you apart from students whose stronger deciles helped tip predictions favorably. To boost your future bar exam chances, focus on realistic steps like targeted LSAT retakes if eligible to push your score higher, or building proven study habits that could have elevated those school deciles—habits that will directly aid bar prep. With dedication to intensive bar review courses and practice exams, you can overcome these predictors and succeed where the model sees risk. Keep pursuing your goals; many students turn similar profiles around with focused effort.
